prior_notes.text: 57-year-old woman for annual exam. No GI complaints — no rectal bleeding, no change in bowel habits, no unintentional weight loss. Never had a screening colonoscopy; now overdue for average-risk colorectal cancer screening. Bilateral knee osteoarthritis managed with intermittent OTC ibuprofen and occasional low-dose aspirin for aches; no daily antiplatelet or anticoagulant prescribed. No atrial fibrillation, no prior VTE. Referred to GI for screening colonoscopy.

Pre-visit phone intake for screening colonoscopy referral. Patient denies anticoagulant use. States she takes 'a baby aspirin sometimes' and ibuprofen for her knees. No blood thinners, no bleeding disorder, no personal or family history of GI cancer reported on call. Flagged for provider review of periprocedural medication instructions.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Ms. Reyes. You're here so we can get your first screening colonoscopy set up. How are you feeling about it?
MA: Before the doctor gets started, let me grab your vitals — you're 5 foot 4 and 143 pounds, blood pressure 124 over 76, temp 36.8, heart rate 72, breathing 14, and oxygen 98 percent on room air.
PT: Fine, a little nervous about the prep, but okay. My doctor said I'm overdue since I turned fifty.
DR: That's right, and you're average risk, which is good. Before we schedule, I want to go over a few things — especially medications, because some can affect bleeding during the scope. The intake note says you don't take any blood thinners. Is that right?
PT: Right, no blood thinners. I'm not on anything like that.
DR: No warfarin, no apixaban or rivaroxaban, nothing like Eliquis or Xarelto?
PT: No, none of those. My heart's fine — I've never had that irregular heartbeat or a blood clot or anything.
DR: Perfect. Now, it also says you take a baby aspirin sometimes. Tell me about that.
PT: Yeah, just the little 81 ones. Not every day — maybe a few times a month if my knees or my back are really aching. Nobody prescribed it, I just buy it.
DR: Okay, so occasional over-the-counter low-dose aspirin, not a daily heart aspirin. And ibuprofen too?
PT: Ibuprofen for the knees, couple times a week. I've got arthritis in both of them.
DR: Got it. That all fits with what's in your chart. The good news is the occasional aspirin isn't a true blood thinner and shouldn't cause any problem for the procedure. We'll give you prep instructions that tell you when to hold the ibuprofen and aspirin beforehand, just as routine.
PT: So I don't have to do anything special ahead of time?
DR: Nothing unusual — no special bridging or clearance. Any bleeding, weight loss, changes in your bowels?
PT: No, nothing like that. Everything's normal that way.
DR: Great. Then this is a straightforward average-risk screening. We'll schedule it and send your prep.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Reyes is a 57-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for a pre-procedure consult to arrange her first screening colonoscopy (average risk, overdue since age 50). She is asymptomatic: denies rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, or unintentional weight loss. Medication review: she denies any anticoagulant or prescription antiplatelet use and specifically denies warfarin, apixaban, and rivaroxaban. She takes over-the-counter low-dose aspirin (81 mg) only intermittently — a few times per month for musculoskeletal aches, not daily and not physician-directed — and OTC ibuprofen a few times weekly for bilateral knee osteoarthritis. No atrial fibrillation, no prior VTE.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 124/76, SpO2 98% on RA; Ht 163 cm (5'4"), Wt 65 kg (143 lb), BMI 24.5. Recent labs (2026-05-04): hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, platelets 262 x10^3/uL, INR 1.0 — all normal, no bleeding-risk abnormality.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Average-risk colorectal cancer screening — asymptomatic; proceed with screening colonoscopy.
- Anticoagulation review: no anticoagulant and no prescription antiplatelet therapy. Intermittent OTC low-dose aspirin is not an anticoagulant; no anticoagulation hold, bridging, or additional clearance required. Low-dose aspirin may be continued for screening colonoscopy.
- NSAID (ibuprofen) use for knee OA — routine periprocedural hold per standard prep instructions.
- Osteoarthritis — continue current OTC symptom management.
- Colonoscopy scheduling + bowel prep instructions to follow; standard periprocedural medication guidance.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your first screening colonoscopy (average risk)
• A review of your medicines and whether any are blood thinners
• Your knee arthritis medicines

Next steps
• We will schedule your screening colonoscopy and send you prep instructions.
• You are not on any blood thinners, so no special stopping plan or clearance is needed.
• Your occasional baby aspirin is fine and does not affect the procedure; the prep instructions will tell you when to briefly hold your aspirin and ibuprofen beforehand.
• Keep managing your knee arthritis as you have been.
• Call us if you notice rectal bleeding, weight loss, or a change in your bowel habits before the procedure.
